questions.scenario: A small company deploys its Node.js API by opening SSH and running `node server.js`. The API disappears when the session closes, does not return after reboot, and has no agreed restart or log location. Operations needs the machine to own the process lifecycle under a dedicated service user. Which change best meets that requirement?

options.text: Run the command under `nohup` from the deploy account and add a cron `@reboot` entry that starts another copy if needed
Run the app from a reviewed systemd service unit so the machine owns its user, command, environment, logs, and restart behavior
Keep the API inside a persistent terminal multiplexer session and document which operator must reconnect after reboot
Use a boot-time shell script without service state or journal integration, then rely on process searches for recovery

questions.explanation: A reviewed systemd unit satisfies all stated constraints: a dedicated identity, boot integration, explicit environment and command, restart behavior, status, and journal ownership. `nohup`, a terminal multiplexer, or an unmanaged boot script can keep a process running in some circumstances, but none provides the complete lifecycle and evidence contract required here. [The systemd service manual](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.service.html) documents service lifecycle.

questions.scenario: A service works when started manually from `/srv/app/current`, but fails under systemd with `ENOENT: no such file or directory, open './templates/home.html'`. The unit runs the right binary and user but has no working-directory directive. What should be added?

options.text: `WantedBy=/srv/app/current`, because install targets set the process current directory
`After=/srv/app/current`, because ordering directives resolve relative file paths
`WorkingDirectory=/srv/app/current` in `[Service]`, so relative paths start from the release directory
`Restart=always`, because repeated starts eventually inherit the deployer's shell directory

questions.explanation: systemd does not inherit the deployer's current directory. `WorkingDirectory=` makes relative application paths deterministic. `WantedBy=` controls installation into a target, `After=` controls ordering, and restart policy cannot change path resolution. The [systemd execution manual](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.exec.html) documents working directories, users, and environment setup.

questions.scenario: An operator creates `/etc/systemd/system/app.service`, then runs `systemctl start app.service`. The app works now but is absent after the next reboot. A later edit to `ExecStart` is also ignored. Which sequence addresses both mistakes?

options.text: Run `systemctl daemon-reload`, enable the unit for boot, restart it to use the changed command, and verify status and health
Run `systemctl reload app.service` because reload always rereads unit files and enables boot links
Run `journalctl --vacuum-time=1d` because old journal entries prevent new unit definitions from loading
Run `systemctl is-enabled` because querying enablement creates the boot symlink and replaces the process

questions.explanation: `daemon-reload` refreshes systemd's unit definitions, `enable` creates the boot relationship, and `restart` launches a new process from changed service instructions. Reload is service-specific and does not generally reread systemd unit metadata or enable boot. Queries and journal cleanup do not mutate lifecycle state. The [systemctl manual](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemctl.html) defines these verbs.

questions.scenario: An app must start after a local credential sidecar, and startup should fail if that sidecar cannot be started. The current unit has only `After=credential-sidecar.service`; on some boots the sidecar is not included in the transaction. Which relationship is appropriate?

options.text: Use only `Wants=credential-sidecar.service`, because a soft dependency also guarantees ordering and blocks app startup on failure
Use only `After=credential-sidecar.service`, because ordering automatically starts the named unit
Use `Before=credential-sidecar.service`, because clients must start before the dependency they consume
Use `Requires=credential-sidecar.service` plus `After=credential-sidecar.service` to pull in the hard dependency and order the app after it

questions.explanation: `Requires=` makes the sidecar part of the startup transaction and treats it as load-bearing. `After=` supplies the separate ordering edge. `Wants=` is softer and does not block the requester when the wanted unit fails, while `After=` alone does not pull anything in. The [systemd unit manual](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.unit.html) documents dependencies and ordering.

questions.scenario: A restart command returns without an error, but the application health check fails seconds later. `systemctl status` reports `Active: failed`, and the latest journal entry says `Error: missing DATABASE_URL`. What is the most useful next action?

options.text: Keep restarting until one process inherits the missing variable from an operator's shell
Fix the service's environment source or permissions, restart once, then verify status, journal output, and health
Enable the unit again because boot enablement injects application environment variables at runtime
Clear the journal because lifecycle errors remain active until their log lines are deleted

questions.explanation: systemd accepting a restart request does not prove the new process reached a healthy state. The journal identifies the missing runtime setting, so fix the environment contract and verify the new process. Repeated restarts, enablement, or deleting evidence do not supply the variable. The [journalctl manual](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/journalctl.html) documents unit and time filtering.

questions.scenario: A unit uses `Restart=on-failure`, `RestartSec=5s`, `StartLimitIntervalSec=60`, and `StartLimitBurst=5`. A bad deploy exits with status 1 repeatedly, then status shows `Result: start-limit-hit`. What should the operator do?

options.text: Change to `Restart=always` so the service bypasses the start-rate limit and hides the failed state
Increase CPUQuota because every start-limit event is caused by scheduler starvation
Inspect the journal for the first failure, fix the root cause, then use `reset-failed` and start the repaired unit
Disable the journal because recording each crash consumes the allowed restart burst

questions.explanation: The start limit stops a tight crash loop so operators can inspect the first useful error. Fixing the cause comes before clearing the failed marker. More aggressive restart policy or unrelated CPU settings would keep the broken release spinning. The [systemd service manual](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.service.html) documents restart policies and rate limiting.

questions.scenario: A Node service normally uses `220M`, peaks near `380M`, and leaked memory once consumed the whole VM. The unit adds `MemoryMax=512M`, `CPUQuota=80%`, and `LimitNOFILE=8192`. After `daemon-reload`, the running process still reports its old file limit. Why?

options.text: The service process must be restarted because execution limits are applied when systemd launches the process
`LimitNOFILE` applies only to timer units, so a service can never receive the configured limit
The limit becomes active only after the next DNS lookup because open files are network resources
The value must be placed in `[Install]` because runtime resource controls are boot relationships

questions.explanation: `daemon-reload` updates systemd's definitions but does not replace the already running process. Restarting applies new execution limits to the launched process and its service cgroup, after which `/proc/<pid>/limits` and `systemctl show` can verify them. The [systemd execution manual](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.exec.html) documents process limits and cgroup controls.

questions.scenario: A team is replacing a daily shell-history cleanup command with systemd. Which design and verification steps are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: Keep the cleanup process running forever with `Type=simple`, even though it does one finite task and exits
Use a `Type=oneshot` service for the finite cleanup command and test that service directly before scheduling it
Use a timer with `OnCalendar=` and `Persistent=true` when a missed run after downtime should execute after boot
Enable only the service unit because services automatically infer daily schedules from their Description field
Enable and start the timer, inspect `list-timers`, and read the oneshot service journal for run results

questions.explanation: A finite job fits a oneshot service, while the timer owns its calendar schedule. `Persistent=true` can catch up a missed calendar run after downtime. Enabling and inspecting the timer plus reading the service journal verifies both scheduling and execution. A Description field does not create a schedule. The [systemd timer manual](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.timer.html) documents these behaviors.
